Using Custom Queries to Create Custom Source Data Items

You can add, rename, and build custom query expressions, and delete custom queries in a data source.

You can build custom query expressions to create custom source data items for an external data source system.
Custom source data items help you to prepare and optimize the data structure of the external source system for
data extraction operations required by the BI Gateway model.

Adding Custom Queries to a Data Source

You can add any number of custom queries to a data source.

To add a custom query to a data source

1.  Click the Custom Queries tab and then click Edit. The Custom Queries page appears.

2.

In the Queries area, click the Add icon to add a custom query. A custom query with a default name is added
in the Queries pane. You can update the default query name.

For more information about renaming a custom query, see Renaming a Custom Query.

3.  Click Save.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 41

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Data Sources

Deleting a Custom Query

You can delete a custom query in the Queries pane. After you save and check in the data source, the system
shows a warning message about the dependent dimensions and measures that could be affected by the custom
query deletion.

To delete a custom query

1.  Under the Data Sources column, click the data source of which you want to delete the custom query. The

data source editor appears.

2.  Click the Custom Queries tab and then click the Edit icon. The Custom Queries page appears.

3.  Under the Queries area, select the Custom Queries that you want to delete.

4.  Click the Delete icon. A confirmation message appears.

5.  Click Delete.

Renaming a Custom Query

The custom query name must be unique within the data source object. The custom query name:

•  Can have a maximum length of 100 characters.

•  Follows the Microsoft SQL Server naming conventions.

•  Follows the BI Gateway database restrictions and should not be a BI Gateway database reserved name.

•  Is case insensitive.

•  Must have a letter or an underscore (_) as the first character.

To rename a custom query

1.

2.

In the Data Source editor, click the Custom Queries tab and then click the Edit icon. The Custom Queries
page appears.

In the Queries area, click the custom query that you want to rename, and then type a new name for the
custom query.

3.  Click Save.

For more information about Microsoft SQL Server naming conventions, see the Microsoft SQL Server user
documentation.

Building a Custom Query Expression to Create Custom Source Data Items

You can build a custom query expression to create custom source data items from the external data source
system. Custom source data items help you to prepare and optimize the data structure of the external source
system for data extraction operations required by the BI Gateway model.

For example, you can build a custom query to combine the information from multiple source data items into a
single one such as merging shift descriptions with the operational schedule.

To build a custom query expression

1.  Under the Data Sources tab, click the data source for which you want to build a custom query expression.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 42

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Data Sources

The data source editor appears.

2.  Click the Custom Queries tab and then click the Edit icon. The Custom Queries page appears. The Available
Source Data Items pane shows the source data items of the current data source. You can expand the source
data items to view the fields, along with the field type, within the source data item.

3.

In the Queries area, click a custom query for which you want to build a custom query expression.

4.

In the Available Source Data Items area, double-click the source data item field you want to use to build the
custom query expression. The selected source data item field is added to the Query Expression editor in the
<Source Data Item Name>.<Field Name> format.

You can build a custom query expression using languages specific to the chosen data source. For example, a
SQL statement for the Microsoft SQL Server Database adapter.

5.  Click Validate to manually validate the custom query expression syntax.

6.  You can slide to enable the Preview Data option and if the query validation succeeds, the data preview area

below the Query Expression editor shows the top ten data rows from the data source.

You can use a valid JSON expression to create a custom query.

Note:
- A JSON expression must have "QueryName". This is a mandatory parameter name. Query name denotes
the name of the custom query defined under the Azure function.
- The other common parameter is "SelectColumn" and is an optional parameter to specify required columns.
- The query returns all the properties (columns) when it is not specified in the query expression or when it is
specified such as SelectColumn: ["*"].

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 43

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Data Sources

- Apart from the "QueryName" and "SelectColumn" parameter, other parameters are query specific
parameter(s).
- Query expression is case in-sensitive.

7.  Click Save. The system automatically validates the custom query syntax after you save the data source.

Note: A data source custom query must not include an ORDER BY clause in the syntax and a semicolon (;) at the
end of the syntax. Although the validation and data preview succeed, data will not be processed at run time.

Using BI Gateway Keywords in a Custom Query

Two special BI Gateway keywords are available for you to use in custom queries:

•  [[RequestedStartTime]]: This is evaluated by the data adapter based on the requested start time from the BI

Gateway Server.

•  [[RequestedEndTime]]: This is evaluated by the data adapter based on the requested end time from the BI

Gateway Server.

NOTE: Custom queries built with these keywords are used for measure calculations.
The Incremental Update feature within dimensions also supports the Custom Query leveraging
[RequestedStartTime] and [RequestedEndTime] parameters.

The BI Gateway Server has a specific requested start and end time based on the time-slicing calculation that is
made by using the Measure object’s configuration.

The following example queries show how to use the keywords for the different adapters. It is important to note
some of the necessary conversion methods, such as the TO_TIMESTAMP for the Oracle data adapter.

Example query for the AVEVA Historian database:

SELECT TagName AS HistTag, Value AS Value, DateTime AS DateTime2 FROM History

WHERE TagName LIKE '%ProductionAbs'

-- DateTime clause for the Run-Time parameters---

AND DateTime >= [[RequestedStartTime]]
AND DateTime < [[RequestedEndTime]]

Example query for a Microsoft SQL Server database:

SELECT TagName AS HistTag, Value AS Value, DateTime AS DateTime2 FROM History

WHERE TagName LIKE '%ProductionAbs'

-- DateTime clause for the Run-Time parameters---

AND DateTime >= [[RequestedStartTime]]
AND DateTime < [[RequestedEndTime]]

Example query for a text file:

SELECT TagName AS HistTag, Value AS Value, DateTime AS DateTime2 FROM History
WHERE TagName LIKE '%ProductionAbs'
-- DateTime clause for the Run-Time parameters---
AND DateTime >=
CDATE((DATEVALUE(LEFT([[RequestedStartTime]],10)))+(TIMEVALUE(MID([[RequestedStartTime]],12
,8))))
AND DateTime <
CDATE((DATEVALUE(LEFT([[RequestedEndTime]],10))) +
(TIMEVALUE(MID([[RequestedEndTime]],12,8))))

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 44

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Data Sources

Using Stored Procedures in a Custom Query

You can use stored procedures in a custom query for a SQL Server data source only.

You must use a different syntax to execute stored procedures than you would for regular custom queries. This is
due to the different execution paths that occur when the query is executed against the data source. The stored
procedure syntax:

•  Must start with the EXEC or EXECUTE statement. Spaces and line breaks preceding this keyword are allowed,

however.

•  May take zero or multiple parameters.

•  Is case-insensitive unless the database itself is set to be case-sensitive.

The following is a list of examples:

EXEC usp_storedProcName
EXEC dbo.usp_storedProcName 1, 'abc'
EXEC myDb.dbo.usp_storedProcName @param1 = 1, @param2 = 'abc'
EXEC myDb..usp_storedProcName @param2 = 'abc', @param1 = 1
EXECUTE usp_storedProcName 1, NULL
EXECUTE usp_storedProcName 1, 'abc' WITH RECOMPILE

Stored procedures can also use the [[RequestedStartTime]] and [[RequestedEndTime]] keywords in place of their
parameter values. This allows the value to be dynamic. Two examples are:

EXEC usp_storedProcName @param1 = [[RequestedStartTime]]
EXEC usp_storedProcName @param1 = [[RequestedStartTime]], @param2 = [[RequestedEndTime]]

For more information on these keywords, see "Using BI Gateway Keywords in a Custom Query".

SQL comments, such as ones starting with a double hyphen (-- comment) or within comment blocks (/*…*/), that
precede the EXEC statement are not supported. For example, the following query will fail because the query
does not start with the EXEC statement:

-- Code Comment;

EXEC usp_storedprocName

You will get an error or invalid syntax message if you create an invalid query or try to use an EXEC statement with
a data adapter other than the SQL Server data adapter. A warning will also be logged to the ArchestrA Logger.

If you change a stored procedure used by a deployed data source, you must redeploy the data source.

Required Modifications to Stored Procedures

There are two scenarios that require a slight modification to stored procedures:

•  Stored procedures that use temporary tables

•  Stored procedures that return a different schema depending on the value of the passed parameter

For these scenarios, use the SET FMTONLY OFF setting at the top of the stored procedure that your custom query
will call.

For example:

CREATE PROCEDURE usp_Sample
( @x varchar(10) )
AS

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 45

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Data Sources

SET FMTONLY OFF;
... --body of procedure

By adding the SET FMTONLY OFF setting at the top of the stored procedure, temporary tables can be used and
only the schema that is retrieved depending on the parameter value will be returned by the data adapter. For
more information, see "Limitations When Using Stored Procedures".

Limitations When Using Stored Procedures

The following limitations apply to stored procedures in custom queries.

•  The stored procedure must return at least an empty record set. If the stored procedure does not return
anything, (not even an empty record set), then the data source preview will be empty and the following
message appears: "The Stored Procedure that was used in the custom query did not return any schema
information. Try running the stored procedure directly to ensure that it returns at least an empty recordset."
At run time, the same message appears in the System Management Console Logger.

•  Stored procedures that use "out" parameters are not supported.

•  Stored procedures that return multiple result sets are not supported.

•  For the result set data, some SQL Server data types are not supported or a data type conversion is performed

by the data adapter. For more information, see "Supported Data Types for Stored Procedures"

•  Stored procedures that use temporary tables are supported if they include the SET FMTONLY OFF setting at

the top of the stored procedure. For an example, see "Required Modifications to Stored Procedures".

•  If the SET FMTONLY OFF setting is not included, the BI Gateway data adapter will return an error within the
IDE during the preview. If the custom query is executed at run time, the ArchestrA Logger will also log an
error with a message indicating that the <name of the temporary table> cannot be found.

•  Stored procedures that have multiple branches (multiple IF statements)—where one branch returns a

different schema than another branch—are not supported unless the stored procedure can be modified or if
only the first branch is exercised when the stored procedure executes.

To illustrate an example of a stored procedure that has multiple branches where each branch returns a different
schema, examine the code below:

IF @X = 'Good'

SELECT * FROM Production_Good

ELSE

SELECT * FROM Production_Bad

In this case, the data adapter query succeeds if one of the following conditions are true:

•  If the custom query passes @x = 'Good'. This query would succeed because passing 'Good' as the value to

parameter @x would exercise the first returned result set. If, however, another value would be passed, then
the ELSE block would get executed. In this case, you would get an error stating that the Column name or
number of supplied values does not match the table definition. This is because the OLEDB data adapter sets
the FMTONLY setting to ON, which retrieves all result sets for all branches and the OLEDB data adapter will
only accept the first result set as its returned schema definition. Therefore we recommend that you do not
use stored procedures that return different result sets depending on which branch was executed.

•  If the SET FMTONLY OFF setting is included at the top of the stored procedure, then either branch can be

executed successfully and this limitation is resolved. For more information, see "Required Modifications to
Stored Procedures".

•  Stored procedures that include the WITH RESULT SETS option are not supported.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 46

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Data Sources

•  Stored procedures that include the USE clause are not supported.

Supported Data Types for Stored Procedures

When a stored procedure returns data through the data adapter, not all SQL Server data types are supported.
The following table indicates which data types are supported:

If a data type is supported, the "Converted into" column provides information about whether the data adapter
would convert the data into a different type, including the length or precision. Depending on the data type, some
supported data types may be truncated to 4000 characters or an error may occur if 4000 characters are
exceeded.

If a data type is not supported, a conversion-type error such as the following may appear: "An error occurred in
AdapterBase::GetDataItemData - ...Implicit conversion from data type hierarchyid to nvarchar is not allowed. Use
the CONVERT function to run this query."

Creating an Azure Function for Custom Query

Apart from the above-mentioned Azure Function for custom queries, you can define new Azure Function for
custom queries.

You can use the the following AVEVA Insight data adapter parameters available in the Azure functions to build
new Azure function for custom query.

Parameter

req.body.MhUrl

req.body.MhKey

Description

AVEVA Insight source URL.

Token to connect the AVEVA Insight source.

req.body.MhSelectString

Array of column name.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 48

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Data Sources

req.body.MhDateTokenFilterString

A valid UTC Date time filter string.

req.body.MhAdditonalFilterString

req.body.MhTask

An additional filter string which could be provided in
the custom query expression from the BI Gateway
Custom query expression.

Represents task name as "getSchema"or "getdata"or
"getnextpagedata"

req.body.MhQueryName

A valid azure function query name

req.body.MhRetrievalMode

Retrieval mode to be used with the Azure function
custom query

req.body.MhTop

Numeric value to fetch top 10 records.

To add new Azure function

Note: You must have a valid Azure subscription to access and create Azure functions.

1.  Log in to the https://portal.azure.com/ using your valid credentials.

2.  Add a new javascript file using the function app wizard.

3.

In the added javascript file, import common libraries and common components.

var request = require('request'); // Library to make asynchronous request

:

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 49

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Data Sources

var helper = require('./helper'); // Common component

4.  Make sure that the function that you create defines a logic to retrieve entity name and could be used to

retrieve the schema of the entity type. For example:

/*
<Summary>
Retrieves Entity name
</summary>
*/
module.exports.GetManagedHistorianEntityName = function () {
return 'Tag';
}

The code sample in the above example use ‘Tag’ entity type.

5.  Update the javascript file with the relevant code to retrieve data from the AVEVA Insight source. For

example:

module.exports.getSampleTags = function (context, req) {
var urlstring = req.body.MhUrl + '/Tags?$select=' + req.body.MhSelectString;
if (req.body.MhTop != null && req.body.MhTop != undefined
&& req.body.MhTop != "") {
urlstring += "&$top=" + req.body.MhTop;
}
var options = {
url: urlstring,
headers: {
"Authorization": req.body.MhKey
},
method: "GET"
};
request(options, (err, result) => {
if (err) {
context.log(err);
}
context.res = result.body;
context.done();
});
}

The above sample code shows how to retrieve tags data from the AVEVA Insight source.

6.  Save and close the javascript file once you have defined your Azure function custom query.

7.  Add a reference to the javascript file in the Index.js file available in the Intelligence folder and make the

following changes as shown in the screenshot:

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 50

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Data Sources

8.  The added Azure function is now available in the Custom Query editor of the BI Gateway Web Model

Configuration application.

NOTE: Make sure you publish the Azure function to your own environment before you can use. You must
also update the OnlineInsight.DataAdapter.config file on the BI Gateway computer to point to their Azure
function URL when you create your own Azure function.

Azure Function Custom Queries for the AVEVA Insight Data Source

An Azure function is used to transform the data from AVEVA Insight to BI Gateway. By default, the following
common custom queries defined in the Azure function are available for use in the Custom Query expression
editor.

about the Tag Extended Property, see the AVEVA Insight Help.

The AVEVA Insight Adapter processes the data returned by the ProcessValues endpoint. The processing is based
on the rule that the timestamp of the current record of the same FQN is considered as the previous record's end
time (EndDateTime field from the table below), and the previous record's timestamp is considered as the current
record's start time (StartDateTime field from the table below). For example, the data shown in the above table is
processed by the AVEVA Insight Adapter and is returned as follows.

Query Expression Operators

You can use the following operators in a query filter expression.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 67

Operator

Equal

Not equal

Starts with

Ends with

Contains

Or

And

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Data Sources

Sample

"Filter":"FQN eq 'Weather.Los Angeles.Temperature'"

"Filter":"FQN ne 'Weather.Los Angeles.Temperature'

"Filter":"startswith(FQN, 'Weather')"

"Filter":"endswith(FQN, 'Temperature')"

"Filter":"contains(FQN,'Temperature')"

"Filter":"FQN eq 'Weather.Los Angeles.Temperature' or
FQN eq 'Weather.Chicago.Temperature'"

"Filter":"FQN eq 'Weather.Los Angeles.Temperature'
and Location eq '/Los Angeles'"

Note:
- If you apply the filter on a String column, ensure that you enclose the String value in Single quotes, as
mentioned in the examples above. However, if you apply the filter on a non-string column (such as decimal or
integer), you can enter the values as-is, without using Single quotes. For example, "Filter": "OpcQuality eq 192"

- AVEVA Insight does not support compound expressions with more than 3 conditions. An example of a
compound filter expression is:
"Filter":"(FQN eq 'Weather.Los Angeles.Temperature' and Location eq '/Los Angeles') OR (FQN eq 'Weather.Los
Angeles.Temperature' and Location eq '/houston') OR (FQN eq 'Weather.Los Angeles.Temperature' and Location
eq '/San Francisco')"

Time Zone for a Data Source

You can define a time zone for a data source to handle the time difference between the BI Gateway Server and
the database that the external data source system is referencing.

The BI Gateway server converts the extracted timestamp data to Coordinated Universal Time (UTC) along with
the offset time of the data source.

The BI Gateway server uses UTC for all run time executions.

A dimension containing fields of DateTime data type is also stored in UTC along with the data source offset and
the local time of the BI Gateway server. This means that for each DateTime or DateTime2 field in the dimension,
the dimension table has separate columns to store data for UTC <TimeFieldName_UTC>, offset time
<TimeFieldName_Offset>, and local time of the BI Gateway server <TimeFieldName>.

For example, a dimension named "DimensionShift" that has DateTime fields defined as "ShiftStartTime" and
"ShiftEndTime" has the following columns:

•  ShiftStartTime

•  ShiftStartTime_UTC

•  ShiftStartTime_Offset

•  ShiftEndTime

•  ShiftEndTime_UTC

•  ShiftEndTime_Offset

The measure table stores the measure calculation period start and end time in UTC, offset time, and the local
time of the BI Gateway server.

For example, a measure has the following fields:

•  "aaValuePeriodStartTime"

•  "aaValuePeriodStartTime_UTC"

•  "aaValuePeriodStartTime_Offset"

•  "aaValuePeriodEndTime"

•  "aaValuePeriodEndTime_UTC"

•  "aaValuePeriodEndTime_Offset"

Retrieve the schema

You can retrieve the source data items using the data source connection information using the Connect button. If
you have defined any custom queries, the Connect button will re-compute the custom queries source data items.

Retrieval operations in order of execution are:

•  Clearing the cached version of the schema within the data source.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 76

•  Retrieving the source data item names from the configured source.

•  Retrieving schema field(s) one source data item at a time.

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Data Sources

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 77

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Dimensions

Dimensions

A dimension provides contextual information to measures in the BI Gateway model. Each dimension typically
stores related information. For example, a product dimension can store the product ID, product name, and
product description. Product ID, product name, and product description are the fields of the product dimension.
Dimension fields are represented by columns of the dimension table in the BI Gateway datastore. You can map
the dimension fields to source data item fields.

In the BI Gateway model, you must provide at least one dimension key for each dimension. The dimension key is
a field or a group of fields that uniquely identifies each record of a dimension. You can include a maximum of
eight fields in a given dimension key. It is critical to ensure that your dimension key combination will generate
unique records. If a duplicable row is found during execution, the complete dimension will be invalidated.

You must specify a refresh rate for a dimension. The BI Gateway services extract data from the data sources,
transform it, and populate the respective dimension table. Thereafter, the dimension is periodically refreshed as
per the refresh rate. You can build a filter expression to filter the required transformed data from external data
sources and populate the dimension table in the datastore.

You can add and configure dimension links in a dimension. Dimension links help you relate dimensions between
each other. When defining a measure, you can add linked dimensions to the measure context even if the
measure source data has no direct reference to the linked dimension. Measure source data will be inferred from
the dimension that defined the dimension link.

You can also configure a dimension as time-slicing. Such dimensions define the time periods for which measure
calculations will be computed. For example, a shift schedule or Work Order dimension can be configured as a
time-slicing dimension through the fields that identify the start and end times of the period they represent.

The first record in the dimension table represents an unknown record. The unknown record is created
automatically. It consists of a primary key field equal to 0 and null values in all the associated source data item
fields. The unknown record is used in the following scenarios:

•  At run time, the system looks up for each dimension link and measure relationship with the values of the

source field(s) used in the link or relationship. These values are then associated with a dimension key value.
When the source field value is "Null" or does not exist in the dimension, then the corresponding record or
aggregate will reference the unknown dimension record.

•  When you relate a constant value to the dimension key ("Null" is also a constant), then the constant value is

attached to all of the aggregates from the source system after the calculation of the aggregates. The resulting
dimension key in the measure table points to the relevant dimension record. If the constant value does not
exist in the dimension or is "Null", then the corresponding record will reference the unknown dimension
record.

Managing Dimensions

After you add and configure data sources, you can add and manage dimensions.

The Dimension editor allows you to:

•  Add a Dimension

•  Edit a Dimension

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 78

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Dimensions

•  Deploy and Undeploy a Dimension

•  Delete a Dimension

•  Manage Dimension Fields

•  Manage Dimension Links

•  Configure Refresh Rate of a Dimension

•  Configure Incremental Update of a Dimension

•  Configure Time-slicing of a Dimension

•  Configure Filter for a Dimension

Adding a Dimension

You can add a dimension to the BI Gateway Model.

To add a dimension

1.  Open the BI Gateway Model Builder.

2.  Click the Add (

) icon in the Floating Action Buttons menu and then click the Dimension (

) button. A

new dimension is added in the BI Gateway model with a default name and the Dimension editor appears in
the right pane.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 79

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Dimensions

3.

In the General & Fields section, do the following:

a.  In the Name box, type a new name for the dimension. The dimension name must be unique and can

have a maximum length of 32 characters.

b.

In the Description box, type a description for the dimension. The description can contain a maximum of
1024 characters.

4.

In the Refresh Rate area, configure how often the dimension is refreshed with data.

a.  In the Interval list, select the time interval for the refresh and then click a time unit in the Units list. The

default refresh rate is One (1) day.

The Interval field units' value must comply with the following conventions:

a.  If the Refresh Rate Unit type is Day, then the number must be between 0 and 366.

b.

If the Refresh Rate Unit type is Hour, then the number must be between 0 and 8784.

c.

If the Refresh Rate Unit type is Minute, then the number must be between 0 and 527040.

b.

In the Offset (minutes) list, select an offset time in minutes. The offset time is used to delay the
scheduled data refresh. You can provide a maximum offset time of 59 minutes. The default offset time is
zero minutes.

5.

In the Fields section, click the Edit (
information, refer to Managing Dimension Fields.

) button to add, edit, or delete dimension fields. For more

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 80

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Dimensions

6.  Click Save.

Editing a Dimension

When editing a dimension, you can:

•  Modify the dimension field names and mappings.

•  Add or delete dimension fields.

•  Modify fields data type.

•  Modify the refresh rate.

•  Re-configure time-slicing.

•  Re-configure dimension links.

•  Re-configure the filter expression.

If you edit and check in a deployed dimension, then the state of the dimension changes to "Pending Changes".
After you edit and check in the dimension, the system shows a message warning about the dependent measures
that can be affected by the modifications.

You can modify the dimension field data type only if the existing data type is implicitly convertible to the new
data type. If the data type is not convertible, then you can delete the existing dimension field and add a new
field.

The following table lists the convertible data types:

Existing field data type

Boolean

Byte

DateTime

DateTime2

Decimal

Double

Int16

Int32

Int64

Guid

String

Other

Convertible to

String

String, Int16, Int32, Int64, Decimal, Double

String

String

String, Double

String

String, Int32, Int64, Decimal, Double

String, Int64, Decimal, Double

String, Decimal, Double

String

String

N/A

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 81

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Dimensions

For more information about run time handling of the data store after modifying deployed dimensions, see
Datastore Updates Resulting from Modifications to Deployed Dimensions.

For more information about run time handling of the on-going processes after modifying deployed dimensions,
see Run time Handling of Deployed BI Gateway Objects.

To edit a dimension

1.  Open the BI Gateway Model Builder.

2.  Under the Dimensions section, click the dimension that you want to edit. The Dimension editor appears with

the selected dimension details.

3.  Do the required modifications.

4.  Click Save.

5.

If the dimension was deployed and changes were saved, it will be marked in the Explorer with a Black tag
indicating that a (re) deployment is requirements may also have affected the dependent measures which will
also be marked.

Important: If you delete a dimension field and check in the dimension, then a confirmation message
appears. After you check in the dimension, the system deletes the column and data of the deleted dimension
field from the dimension table.

Deleting a Dimension

You can delete a dimension that is not deployed or is not referenced by a dependent measure.

To delete a dimension

1.  Open the BI Gateway Model Builder.

2.  Under the Dimension column, click the dimension that you want to delete.

3.  On the Action menu, click the Delete (

) button. A confirmation message appears.

NOTE: The Delete button in the Dimension editor is available only when the selected dimension is
undeployed.

4.  Click Delete.

NOTE: The model integrity validation will first check for dependency relationship and block the deletion if
the dimension is referenced in other objects.

Deploying and undeploying a Dimension

You deploy the dimension to the BI Gateway Server.

To deploy a dimension

1.  Open the BI Gateway Model Builder.

2.  Under the Dimension column, click the dimension that you want to deploy. The Dimension editor appears in

the right pane with the selected dimension details.

3.  On the Action menu, click the Deploy (

) button. A confirmation message appears.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 82

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Dimensions

4.  Click Yes. After the successful deployment the the deploy status next to the dimension name changes from

yellow to green.

NOTE: By default, when you deploy a dimension, it deploy the parent objects too if it is undeployed.

To undeploy a dimension

1.  Open the BI Gateway Model Builder.

2.  Under the Dimension column, click the dimension that you want to undeploy. The Dimension editor appears

in the right pane with the selected dimension details.

3.  On the Action menu, click the Undeploy (

) button. A confirmation message appears.

4.  Click Yes. After the successful undeployment, the deploy status next to the dimension name changes from

green to yellow.

Managing Dimension Fields

You can add, rename, and delete fields in a dimension.

Adding a Dimension Field

A dimension can have a maximum of 190 dimension fields. You must provide at least one dimension key field per
dimension. A dimension key is a field that uniquely identities each record in the dimension. You can provide a
maximum of eight keys fields per dimension. When a dimension key contains more than one field, it is called a
composite key.

To add a dimension field

1.  Open the BI Gateway Model Builder.

2.  Under the Dimensions column, click the dimension to which you want to add a dimension field.

3.

In the Dimension editor in the right pane, click the Fields (

) button. The Fields editor appears.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 83

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Dimensions

4.  Click the Add (

) button. A new row is added in the grid.

5.  Under the Key column, drag to enable the dimension key for the corresponding dimension field. You must
provide at least one dimension key. You can include a maximum of eight fields for the dimension key.

6.  Under the Field name column, type a name for the dimension field.

The dimension field name must comply with the following conventions:

•  The dimension field name is case insensitive and the maximum length is 100 characters.

•  The dimension field name must be unique and can contain characters that are valid in Microsoft SQL
Server for regular column identifiers except that the first character of the name must be a letter as
defined by the latest Unicode Standard, or an underscore (_). The subsequent characters can be letters
or numbers as defined by the latest Unicode Standard, underscore (_), dollar ($), blank space, and hash
(#) characters.

•  You cannot use the word "TimeStamp" for the dimension field name.

7.  Under the Field type column, click a data type for the corresponding dimension field. The default data type

is Boolean. For more information about the data type conversions, see Data type mapping.

8.  Click Save.

Renaming a Dimension Field

If you rename a dimension field, the configurations, mappings, and relationships of the dimension field are not
modified.

To rename a dimension field

1.  Open the BI Gateway Model Builder.

2.  Under the Dimensions column, click the dimension to which you want to add a dimension field.

3.

In the Dimension editor in the right pane, click the Fields (

) button. The Fields editor appears.

4.  Under the Field name column, click the dimension field that you want to rename.

5.  Type a new name for the dimension field and then press Enter.

For more information, see Adding a Dimension Field.

Deleting a Dimension Field

If you delete a dimension field, then the configurations, mappings, relationships, and data of the dimension field
are deleted.

To delete a dimension field

1.  Open the BI Gateway Model Builder.

2.  Under the Dimensions column, click the dimension to which you want to add a dimension field.

3.

In the Dimension editor in the right pane, click the Fields (

) button. The Fields editor appears.

4.  Select the dimension field row that you want to delete, and then click Delete (

). A confirmation message

appears.

5.  Click Yes and then click Save.

Important: If deployed the indicator will show as dirty (black) and that changes may trigger measures to be
updated too.

Selecting Source Data Items for a Dimension

You can select data items from any of the configured data sources to later map to your dimension fields.

The source data items shown in the Select source data items dialog box are retrieved from the saved schema.
You can use the Get schema option in the data source editor (Connection tab) to refresh the data source
schema. For more information, see Configuring the Connection Information for Data Sources.

To select source data items for a dimension

1.  Open the BI Gateway Model Builder.

2.  Under the Dimensions column, click the dimension to which you want to add a dimension field.

3.

In the Dimension editor, click the Fields (

) button. The Fields editor appears.

4.  Click the Data Source Item Picker (

) button. The Data Source Item Picker dialog box appears.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 85

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Dimensions

The Data Source items pane shows the available schema of the data sources.

5.  Click the Expand icon to expand the data source and view the source data items within the data source.

NOTE: You can use the Find box to search a source data item. The search behaves as a 'Contain' query.

6.  Select a source data item from which you want to expose the fields to add the data source item. Deselect a

source data item to move an item back to the Data Source items pane.

The names in a data source table should not contain the special character, semicolon (;). The names that
contain special characters like semicolon (;) and (#) do not appear in data source items pane.

The bottom grid of the dialog box shows the fields of the source data item that you select in the Data Source
items pane. The first source (from the top of the list) providing a value for the field will be used in the
resulting dimension table.

7.  Slide to enable the Preview source data option to view sample data from the data source.

8.  Click OK.

Selected data source items become available for mapping to the dimension fields.

For more information, see Mapping Dimension Fields to Data Item Fields.

Mapping Dimension Fields to Data Item Fields

You can map dimension fields in the BI Gateway model to data source item fields. At run time, the data from the
data source item fields populates the dimension fields according to these mappings.

For more information about data type compatibility, see Data type mapping.

Mapping Dimension Fields to Fields from a Single Source Data Item

You can map dimension fields to source data item fields from a single source data item.

To map dimension fields to fields from single source data item

1.  Open the BI Gateway Model Builder.

2.  Under the Dimensions column, click the dimension to which you want to add a dimension field.

3.

In the Dimension editor in the right pane, click the Fields (

) button. The Fields editor appears.

The Field name column shows the name of the dimension fields.

The Field type column shows the data type of the corresponding dimension fields.

For more information on Managing Dimension Fields, see Managing Dimension Fields.

The column to the right of Field Type shows the source data item that you have selected for the dimension.

For more information, see Selecting Source Data Items for a Dimension.

4.  Under the source data item column, select the source data item fields to map to the corresponding

dimension fields.

You can provide a constant value in the source data item field. After check in the system verifies whether the
dimension field type is compatible with the constant type.

You can map a dimension key field that is part of a composite key to a "Null" or constant value only if at least
one of the key fields of the composite key is mapped to a source data item field. You cannot map a
dimension key field that is part of a composite key to "Skip".

5.  Click Save.

Mapping One Dimension Field to Fields from Multiple Data Items

You can map one dimension field to fields from multiple source data items.

To map one dimension field to fields from multiple source data items

1.  Open the BI Gateway Model Builder.

2.  Under the Dimensions column, click the dimension to which you want to add a dimension field.

3.

In the Dimension editor in the right pane, click the Fields (

) button. The Fields editor appears.

The Field name column shows the name of the dimension fields.

The Field type column shows the data type of the corresponding dimension fields.

The corresponding columns show the source data items with the source data item fields that you have
exposed to the dimension.

For more information, see Selecting Source Data Items for a Dimension.

4.  Under the source data item columns, click the source data item fields to map to the corresponding

dimension field.

The left-most source data item column is of the highest priority. For more information about setting the
source data item priority, see Selecting Source Data Items for a Dimension.

For each of the dimension fields, you can:

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 87

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Dimensions

•  Provide a constant value in the source data item field. During the object save and check in, the system

verifies whether the dimension field type is compatible with the constant type.

•  Specify the value "Skip" in the source data item field. The system skips the source data item for that field
and takes the next highest priority of source data item. If the next highest priority of source data item
does not contain a record for the dimension key, then the value is set to "Null". You cannot map a
dimension key field that is part of a composite key to "Skip".

•  Map a dimension key field that is part of a composite key to a "Null" or constant value only if at least one
of the key fields of the composite key is mapped to a source data item field. You cannot map a dimension
key field that is part of a composite key to "Skip".

5.  Click Save.

Managing Dimension Links

You can add, rename, configure, and delete dimension links. Dimension links allow you to create links between
dimensions. When defining a measure, you can add linked dimensions to the measure context even if the
measure source data has no direct reference to the linked dimension and it will be inferred from the dimension
that defined the dimension link.

For more information, see Defining Relationships for a Measure.

Adding a Dimension Link

You can add a maximum of 30 dimension links in a dimension.

To add a dimension link

1.  Under the Dimensions column, click the dimension for which you want to add a dimension link.

The Dimension editor appears.

2.  Click the Links tab.

The editor pane appears as follows.

) icon to add a dimension link. A dimension link with a default name is

added.

For more information about renaming a dimension link, see Rename a dimension link.

4.  Click Save.

NOTE: You can select a dimension link in the Links area and select Delete to delete the dimension link. After
you save the dimension, the associated measure(s) will show as "Deployed with pending changes" (black
indicator) and will need to be reconfigured.

Rename a dimension link

Dimension link names must be unique within each dimension. A dimension link name can have a maximum
length of 100 characters.

Rename a dimension link

1.  Under the Dimensions column, click the dimension of which you want to rename the dimension link. The

dimension editor appears.

underscore (_), dollar ($), and hash (#) characters.

5.  Click Save.

Configuring a Dimension Link

Dimension links allow you to create links between dimensions. When defining a measure, you can add linked
dimensions to the measure context even if the measure source data has no direct reference to the linked
dimension. The measure source data will be inferred from the dimension that defined the dimension link.

When you configure a link in a dimension and deploy the dimension, the system adds a column in the dimension
table of the current dimension for each of the linked dimensions. This column contains the foreign key ID of the
linked dimension. The column name is the same as the link name.

To configure a dimension link

1.  Under the Dimensions column, click the dimension for which you want to configure a dimension link. The

dimension editor appears.

2.  Click the Links tab.

The editor pane appears as follows.

3.

In the Links section, click a dimension link that you want to configure.

The Link name box shows the dimension link that you select.

The Destination dimension list shows all dimensions but the current dimension of the BI Gateway model.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 90

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Dimensions

4.

In the Destination dimension list, click a dimension that you want to link to the current dimension.

The Destination key field column in the Mappings grid shows all the key fields of the linked dimension.

The Dimension field column in the Mappings grid shows the fields of the current dimension.

5.  Under the Dimension field column, select a dimension field that you want to map to the corresponding

dimension key field. You must provide a mapping for all the corresponding key fields of the linked dimension.
Be sure that the field data type of the current dimension is compatible with the key field data type of the
linked dimension. For more information, see Implicitly convertible data types.

NOTE: When deploying dimensions referring to 'linked dimensions', ensure that the linked dimensions are
deployed before the dimension using those links so that the relationships can be found. In the same way, the
linked dimensions should be refreshed at a faster rate than the dimensions using those references.

6.  Click Save.

Configuring Time-Slicing for a Dimension

You can enable time-slicing for a dimension. Time-slicing defines the time periods for which measure calculations
are aggregated. A measure can be time-sliced by more than one time-slicing dimension. To configure time-slicing
for a dimension, you must define which fields represent the start and end time or start time and duration for
time-slices.

If you have a time-defining dimension that produces time slices that are overlapping, and you are using a time-
based relationship, aggregate calculation results are produced for the time overlaps. There is an aggregate
calculation for each time slice. Be aware that reports based on these aggregate calculations may include the
overlap results and therefore, include duplicated data. This behavior does not occur for key-based relationships.

To configure time-slicing for a dimension

1.  Open the BI Gateway Model Builder.

2.  Click the Add (

) icon in the Fast Action Buttons menu and then click the Dimension (
new dimension is added in the BI Gateway model with a default name and the Dimension editor appears in
the right pane.

) button. A

3.  Click the Advanced tab.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 91

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Dimensions

4.  Select the Time slicing check box to enable time-slicing for the dimension.

5.

In the Start time list, click a field representing the start time of each time-slice of the dimension. The Start
time list shows the DateTime fields of the dimension.

6.  Configure the end time field for time-slices by doing one of the following:

•  In the End time list, click a field representing the end time of each time-slice of the dimension. The End

time list shows the DateTime fields of the dimension.

•  In the Duration list, click a field or type a number representing the duration of each time-slice of the

dimension. The Duration list shows the numeric fields (Int16, Int32, and Int64) of the dimension. Then,
in the Unit list, select a unit of time for the duration. You can provide a minimum period of one
millisecond and a maximum period of 366 days.

7.  Click Save.

Configuring an Incremental Update for a Dimension

By default, all records of a dimension are updated at each refresh execution. However, for dimensions with large
row counts, you may configure the dimension to update incrementally. Incremental updates are based on the
DateTime field of the dimension. The initial population of the dimension only considers records from the data
collection start time and then, at each refresh, from records corresponding to the update period. This limits the
number of records to be processed at each refresh.

Alternatively, when you cannot use an incremental update, you may consider a dimension filter expression to
limit the number of records handled at each refresh.

For example, if you configure a dimension with a refresh rate of 15 minutes and an update period of 1 hour every
15 minutes, the system will query the past hour of data and update the dimension table accordingly.

NOTE: When using the Historian data adapter, make sure that you configure the "Enable incremental update" on
the Advanced tab with a "Data Collection Start" date / time.

To configure an incremental update for a dimension

1.  Open the BI Gateway Model Builder.

2.  Click the Add (

) icon in the Floating Action Buttons menu and then click the Dimension (

) button. A

new dimension is added in the BI Gateway model with a default name and the Dimension editor appears in
the right pane.

3.  Click the Advanced tab.

4.  Select the Incremental update check box. The Time reference field list shows the DateTime fields of the

current dimension.

5.

In the Data collection start list, click a data collection start date and time.

6.

In the Time reference field list, click a dimension DateTime reference field for the update period.

NOTE: This is the field on which the Data collection start time will be applied. The Time reference field list
shows the DateTime fields of the current dimension.

7.

In the Incremental Update Period area, do the following:

a.  In the Interval list, click the required update period for the dimension.

b.

In the Unit list, click the required unit of time for the update period. You can provide a minimum update

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 93

period of one minute and a maximum update period of 366 days. The default update period is one day.

Ensure that the update period is equal to or more than the refresh rate for the dimension.

8.  Click Save.

Measures

Measures

A measure is a group of calculations that shares the same context. A measure consists of calculations for each
source system, time period, and for each unique combination of dimension value.

Measures are typically numerical values computed from source systems and put into context with dimensions.
You can configure measures by defining which calculations need to be computed, along with the context
(dimensions) to which the calculations are related. Upon deployment, a measure is represented by a table in the
datastore, which contains the calculated values along with references to the related dimensions. Each record in
the measure table represents a time-slice (period) for which the calculated values are computed.

Typically measure tables are updated with time, adding records for every time-slice defined by time-slicing
dimensions and the measure period. As such, measure tables are updated incrementally, from the time defined
by the data collection start time.

Because measure records are described by a time-slice, a measure object must at least have one time context
defined either by a measure period, and/or time-slicing dimensions.

You can configure a measure period, which allows the computation of the measure calculations at the specified
period. For example, a measure object configured with a measure period of one hour stores calculations in the
measure table for every hour since the data collection start.

You can also add one or more time-slicing dimensions to the context of a measure. These time-slicing can be
used instead of a measure period. At run time, time periods defined by all time-slicing dimensions and by the
measure period are all combined together to create smaller, contiguous time-slices by which measure
calculations are computed.

For more information about specifying a measure period, see "Configuring a Measure Period".

You have to provide a refresh rate for the measure. The refresh rate recomputes calculations for the current
time-slice and also verifies whether a new time-slice must be defined. The following are three scenarios when
considering the refresh rate and the measure period:

•  The refresh rate is smaller than the measure period or the time-slice.

The measure is updated until a new measure period or time-slice is identified. Thereafter, a new measure
value is calculated.

•  The refresh rate is bigger than the measure period or the time-slice.

The refresh rate verifies whether a new measure period or a time-slice is identified since the last refresh
period. New measure values are calculated for the identified measure period and time-slices.

•  The refresh rate is equal to the measure period or the time-slice.

A new measure value is calculated at the identified measure period and time-slice.

NOTE: The refresh rate just defines how often the calculation is executed. The above scenarios are only the
result of the refresh execution and are not explicitly triggered.

In addition to the refresh rate, the Update Period defines how much data is updated in the measure table. For
example, if you configure a Measure object with a refresh rate of 15 minutes and an update period of 1 hour,
every 15 minutes, the BI Gateway services update the last hour of data in the measure table.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 96

AVEVA™ BI Gateway On-Premises
Measures

•  Define Relationships for a Measure

Adding a Measure

You can create a measure by using the FAB button.

To add a measure

1.  Open the BI Gateway Model Builder.

2.  Click the Add (

) icon in the Fast Action Buttons menu and then click the Measure (

measure is added in the BI Gateway model with a default name and the Measure editor appears in the right

Measures

a.  In the Name box, type a new name for the measure. The measure name must be unique and can have a
maximum length of 32 characters. The measure name can contain only a-z, A-Z, 0-9, underscore (_),
dollar ($), and hash (#) characters. The first character must not be a dollar sign ($). The measure name
must contain at least one letter.
